# Results

The analytical corpus comprises 6,833 emails from the inbox of a central CITF Databank
coordinator, spanning correspondence with 100 participating studies from February 2021
to September 2025, involving 488 unique senders. NMF identified 14 topics organized
into four thematic categories. The 14 topics identified by the model and their five
most characteristic terms are shown in @fig-keywords. The x-axis reflects each term's
NMF component weight — a non-negative score indicating the strength of association
between that term and the topic, derived from the factorization of the Term
Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency (TF-IDF) matrix. Higher scores indicate terms
that are more strongly and distinctively associated with that topic relative to others
in the vocabulary.

![Top 5 characteristic terms and topic coherence scores (c_v) for each of the 14 NMF topics.](visuals/fig2_keywords.png){#fig-keywords width=70%}

The topic labels, thematic groupings, and descriptions generated through LLM-assisted
annotation are shown in @fig-dendrogram.

![LLM-assisted labels, thematic groupings, and descriptions for the 14 NMF topics.](visuals/fig3_dendrogram.png){#fig-dendrogram}

## Co-occurrence Analysis of Topic Relationships

Topic co-occurrence analysis revealed two largely separate clusters of topics within the
corpus, with Agreements and Contracts (5 topics, 3,793 multi-topic email assignments),
Data Operations (4 topics, 2,772), Study Documentation (3 topics, 2,602), and
Coordination (2 topics, 1,612) (see @fig-cooccurrence). Core governance topics —
Document Signatures, Document Appendices, and DSA Status — co-occurred frequently
with each other (co-occurrence scores: 0.19--0.28) but rarely with core technical topics
such as Data Upload Instructions, Variable Mapping, and Data Transfer Initiation
(co-occurrence scores: 0.02--0.09). This structural separation suggests that governance
and data operations workstreams ran largely in parallel rather than in close coordination.

![Topic co-occurrence matrix (0--0.30) with associated number of emails via multi-topic assignment.](visuals/fig4_cooccurrence.png){#fig-cooccurrence}

The primary point of convergence between these two clusters was Document Review and
Comments, the most frequently assigned topic in the corpus (1,323 email assignments)
and the most broadly connected across all thematic categories (co-occurrence scores:
0.10--0.24). This topic likely reflects the reconciliation of technical data descriptions
and management requirements with institutional expectations, as data sharing agreements
needed to stipulate the exact data being shared and the conditions under which they
would be managed. It represents the interface between governance and operations —
the point at which these concerns were most likely to be addressed together.

## Topic Distribution Across Lifecycle Phases and Sender Roles

While dominant topics shifted clearly across phases of the data sharing lifecycle,
governance and administrative concerns proved more persistent than the phase structure
alone would suggest (see @fig-phase-sender). In early phases, correspondence was
dominated by document work: DSA Status accounted for 12% of emails and Document
Review for 12% in the Intro-to-DSA phase. In later phases, technical topics came to
the fore: Data Upload Instructions accounted for 27% of emails in the
Upload-to-Catalogue phase, and Serology and Data Harmonization for 30% in the
Catalogue-to-Publication phase.

![Topic theme assignments by lifecycle phase and sender category.](visuals/fig5_phase_sender.png){#fig-phase-sender}

However, multi-topic analysis showed that governance and administrative concerns did
not recede as the lifecycle progressed. Even in the Catalogue-to-Publication phase —
dominated by Serology and Data Harmonization — DSA Status and Amendments (5%),
Document Review (7%), and Document Signatures (5%) together still accounted for 17%
of emails. Rather than being treated as an initial phase with a definite endpoint, data
governance concerns persisted throughout the data sharing lifecycle, requiring ongoing
attention well beyond the execution of formal agreements.

Phase duration data further illustrate where effort was concentrated. Studies spent a
median of 395 days in DSA negotiation (Intro-to-DSA phase) and 398 days in data
preparation (DSA-to-Upload phase), compared to only 22 days for technical cataloguing
(Upload-to-Catalogue phase) — an 18:1 ratio of administrative to technical time.

Sender analysis revealed sharply uneven operational engagement across institutional
roles. Databank teams were substantially more active as email senders than
representatives of partner studies. The Harmonization Group sent emails at a ratio of
115 emails per sender, and the Secretariat at 101 emails per sender, compared to 7
emails per sender among External Partners and 5 among Study Investigators. This
concentration of email activity within Databank teams makes visible the coordination
labour that is sustained by a small number of highly active individuals.

The topical focus of each sender group also differed markedly. The Harmonization Group
concentrated over 60% of its emails on two topics — Variable Mapping and Serology and
Data Harmonization — reflecting its specialized focus on the technical core of data
interoperability. The Secretariat's topical profile was the most evenly distributed,
reflecting its broad coordinating mandate across all phases and workstreams. Study
Investigators' most frequent topic was Meeting Scheduling, which co-occurred most
strongly with Document Review (0.19) and DSA Status (0.15), suggesting that
investigators were convened primarily to discuss documentation matters rather than to
participate in technical data operations.

## Gender Distribution of Coordination Labour

Gender analysis revealed a consistent pattern of stratification across both roles and
phases. The proportion of topic assignments attributed to women and men across all
14 topics is shown in @fig-gender. Women comprised 74% of Study Staff senders —
the largest sender group and the one carrying the greatest volume of operational
coordination work — while the Databank Secretariat was the only category where men
were the majority (57%). At the phase level, women-coded senders dominated the
earliest and most labour-intensive phases: Funding to Introduction (69% women) and
Introduction to DSA (63% women). The final publication phase was the most
male-dominated (67% men). This progression — from female-dominated early phases
involving onboarding and negotiation, to male-dominated later phases associated with
publication — is consistent with prior research documenting the feminization of
invisible coordination labour in research data infrastructure [@choroszewicz2022].

![Topic assignments by gender category.](visuals/fig6_gender.png){#fig-gender width=70%}
